A History of Mind

Antiquity Late Antiquity South Asian East Asian Islamic Jewish Scholasticism
Renaissance Early Modern
19th Century Phenomenology Analytic
Consciousness Science Machine Minds Moral Status
Glossary About
Medieval Latin Scholasticism

Thomas Aquinas

1225 – 1274

Soul as the substantial form of a human being: the great synthesis of Aristotle, Augustine, and Christian revelation.

Aquinas does not write a modern “philosophy of mind” in the narrow post-Cartesian sense. He writes a metaphysics of life, soul, intellect, will, knowledge, divine cognition, angelic cognition, and human action. But if one extracts a philosophy of mind from those materials, the result is remarkably systematic: mind is not a private inner theatre, not a brain process, not a second complete substance glued to the body, and not merely “consciousness.” It is a graded order of cognitive and appetitive powers rooted in form, actuality, intentionality, and degrees of immateriality. Human mind is an embodied intellectual life; angelic mind is finite disembodied intellect and will; divine mind is not a faculty at all but God’s own simple act of being, knowing, and willing. Aquinas’s doctrine is therefore not merely “psychology plus theology”: his accounts of God and angels function as limiting cases that clarify what cognition is, what human cognition is not, and why embodiment matters.

The central thesis can be put this way: to be cognitive is to be able, in some mode, to receive or possess the form of another without becoming that other materially; the higher the immateriality of the knower, the more universal, unified, and actual its knowledge. Human beings stand at an intermediate point: the human soul is incorporeal and subsistent in virtue of intellectual operation, yet it is naturally the form of a body and normally thinks only by turning to sensory images, or phantasmata. Aquinas’s system is therefore best called hylomorphic dualism, embodied intellectualism, or non-reductive form-matter realism, rather than Cartesian substance dualism or materialism. Aquinas defines soul as the first principle of life and argues that the human intellectual principle, called mind or intellect, has an operation not exercised through a bodily organ, while also insisting that the human being is not the soul alone but the composite of soul and body.

1. Method: what counts as “mind” in Aquinas

The word “mind” has to be handled carefully. Aquinas’s Latin vocabulary is not identical to modern English usage. Anima means soul, the principle of life in plants, animals, and humans. Mens can mean the higher intellectual part of the soul, often including intellect and will under Augustinian influence. Intellectus is intellect, the power by which universals and essences are understood. Ratio is reason, not a separate faculty from intellect, but intellect operating discursively, moving from one thing known to another. Voluntas is will, the intellectual appetite. Species is a formal likeness or intelligible form by which something is known; it is not normally the object known. Phantasma is the sensory image or internally retained sensory configuration on which human thinking depends in this life. Aquinas distinguishes reason and intellect as modes of one power, and Stanford’s contemporary summary is right to stress that the immaterial human mind in Aquinas is not “just the intellect,” but agent intellect and possible intellect working with will.

Aquinas’s method is also not introspective in the modern Cartesian sense. He does not begin from indubitable private consciousness. He begins from living beings and their operations. Powers are known through acts, and acts through objects. A power is specified by what it is for: sight by color, intellect by being or intelligible nature, will by good. That is why the study of mind must include ontology, psychology, epistemology, action theory, and metaphysics of God. Aquinas explicitly says the soul’s powers are distinguished by acts and objects, and that the human soul lies on the border of corporeal and spiritual things, possessing powers related to both.

2. The metaphysical ground: soul as form, not ghost

Aquinas’s first decisive move is Aristotelian but transformed by Christian commitments: the soul is not a body; it is the first actuality or form of a living body. It is what makes a living thing to be alive. This applies analogically to plants, animals, and human beings, though the human soul is unique because it includes intellect and will. Aquinas does not mean that a body exists as a complete body and then receives a soul as an added instrument. Rather, the soul is what makes this matter be a living body of this kind. In the human case, the intellectual soul is the substantial form by which the human being lives, senses, moves, understands, and acts.

This is why Aquinas is not a Cartesian dualist. He rejects the idea that the human being is really the soul, with the body as an external instrument. If the intellective principle were not the form of the body, Aquinas argues, it would be hard to explain how understanding is genuinely the act of Socrates rather than the act of some separate intellect associated with Socrates. The same human being is conscious of both sensing and understanding; the body is not an accidental container but part of the human substance.

At the same time, Aquinas is not a materialist. The intellect is not an act of a bodily organ in the way seeing is an act of eye and visual system. His central argument is that a power that can know all bodily natures cannot itself be locked into one determinate bodily nature as its organ; otherwise that bodily determination would obstruct its universal range. The intellectual principle, therefore, is incorporeal and subsistent. The Latin text in the Corpus Thomisticum states that the principle called mens or intellectus has an operation per se in which the body does not share as an organ.

The result is subtle. The human soul is subsistent, but it is not a complete human person by itself. It can exist apart from the body, but its natural perfection is to inform a body. Aquinas therefore treats the separated soul as real but incomplete: it is not an angel, not a whole human being, and not the natural mode of human existence. Contemporary scholarship recognizes a real interpretive debate here over whether Aquinas’s view of post-mortem separated souls implies “survivalism” or a more radical “corruptionist” reading, but Aquinas is unambiguous that the human being in this life is the soul-body composite.

3. One soul, many powers

Aquinas rejects the view that the human being has one soul for nutrition, another for sensation, and another for intellect. The human soul is one substantial form that virtually contains the lower powers. The intellectual soul is not added to an already living animal body; it is the one form by which the human body is bodily, living, sensitive, and rational. This doctrine of the unity of substantial form is crucial for his philosophy of mind because it prevents the fragmentation of the human person into biological organism plus animal psyche plus rational ghost.

The soul itself is not identical with all its powers. Aquinas distinguishes the essence of the soul from its powers because a creature is not pure act: it has capacities that can be actualized. God alone has no such distinction between essence, power, and operation. Human powers include vegetative, sensitive, appetitive, locomotive, and intellectual powers. Some powers, such as nutrition and sensation, belong to the soul-body composite because they require bodily organs. Intellect and will, by contrast, are rooted in the soul alone, though in embodied life intellect depends on sensory materials.

The five broad classes of soul-powers are these. Vegetative powers concern nutrition, growth, and reproduction. Sensitive powers concern external and internal sensation. Appetitive powers concern inclination toward apprehended goods and away from apprehended evils. Locomotive powers concern bodily movement. Intellectual powers concern universal understanding and rational willing. This architecture matters because Aquinas’s “mind” is not a single box. It is an ordered system of powers, some bodily, some non-bodily, some cognitive, some appetitive, but all rooted in one living human form.

4. Perception: cognition begins as intentional reception of form

Aquinas’s theory of cognition begins with a broad metaphysical contrast: non-cognitive things possess only their own forms, whereas cognitive things can in some way possess the forms of other things. This does not mean that the eye becomes literally colored in the same way a wall is colored, or that the intellect becomes materially identical with what it thinks. Cognition involves the reception of form “spiritually” or intentionally. In sensory cognition, a bodily organ is involved; in intellectual cognition, no bodily organ is the subject of the act. SEP summarizes the point by saying that for Aquinas cognition in general works through the reception of the form of the thing perceived or thought, while noting scholarly disagreement over whether this should be read as direct realism or mediated representation.

The five external senses receive proper sensibles such as color, sound, odor, flavor, and tactile qualities. They also perceive common sensibles such as shape, size, number, motion, and rest, though these are not proper to any one sense. But sensory experience is not exhausted by five channels. Aquinas assigns great importance to the internal senses, which use bodily organs and are indispensable for animal behavior and human thought. Contemporary summaries rightly note that these internal senses are brain-involving powers, even though the intellect itself is not a brain power.

Aquinas usually identifies four internal senses: common sense, imagination or phantasy, the estimative power in animals or cogitative power in humans, and memory. Common sense unifies the deliverances of external senses and makes possible awareness such as seeing that one sees. Imagination retains sensible forms when the object is absent. The estimative or cogitative power grasps “intentions” not reducible to proper sensibles: the sheep does not merely see gray shape and motion but apprehends the wolf as enemy; the bird apprehends straw as useful for nesting. In humans this becomes the cogitative power or “particular reason,” which compares individual intentions and is deeply involved in practical intelligence. Memory retains these intentions.

This is one of the most underappreciated parts of Aquinas’s philosophy of mind. He has no crude empiricism in which sensation is raw data and intellect alone supplies meaning. The internal senses already structure experience in practical and quasi-interpretive ways. But he also has no reduction of intellect to sophisticated animal perception. The cogitative power can manage particulars; only intellect grasps universals. Human cognition is therefore layered: bodily perception, internal sensory organization, phantasm formation, abstraction, judgment, reasoning, and willing.

5. Phantasms and abstraction: why human thought is embodied

Aquinas’s famous thesis is that in this life the human soul understands nothing actually without phantasms. Even when we think abstractly, we need images, examples, diagrams, remembered cases, or internally formed sensory representations. This is not an incidental weakness; it follows from the proper object of the human intellect, which is the quiddity or nature of material things. The human intellect is immaterial, but it is the intellect of an embodied soul whose natural access to intelligibility begins with sensible things.

The process is not passive copying. The possible intellect begins in potency to intelligible forms, like a blank tablet. The agent intellect is the active intellectual light that makes potentially intelligible contents actually intelligible by abstracting them from phantasms. Aquinas rejects the view that the agent intellect is a separate intelligence supplying human knowledge from outside; it is a power of the human soul, though ultimately derived from God as first source of intellectual light.

The agent intellect does not “manufacture” unreality. It abstracts the universal nature from individuating conditions. A particular dog is here, brown, moving, and sensed under material conditions. The intellect does not attend to the dog as this individual under these conditions, but to the nature dog or animal as intelligible. Aquinas’s formula is not that the universal exists separately in a Platonic heaven; rather, the nature exists in particulars materially and in the intellect immaterially and universally. The Corpus Thomisticum’s text of Summa theologiae I, q. 84, a. 1 states that the intellect receives the species of material and mobile bodies immaterially and immovably according to its own mode; what is received is received according to the mode of the receiver.

This allows Aquinas to avoid both Platonism and nominalism. He need not posit separately existing Forms to explain universal knowledge, but neither does he reduce universals to names. Universal intelligibility is grounded in real natures, abstracted by the intellect from phantasms. This is why Treccani’s Italian account accurately describes Aquinas’s process as sensation unified by internal sense, passed into fantasy as a sensible image, from which the agent intellect abstracts the intelligible and actualizes the possible intellect.

6. Species are not a veil of ideas

Aquinas’s theory of species is easily misunderstood. A species is not normally an inner object that we first inspect before inferring an external object. It is the form or likeness by which the knower is made able to know the thing. Aquinas explicitly denies that the intelligible species is what is understood; it is that by which the intellect understands. If the species were what we primarily knew, then knowledge would be trapped inside mental representations. Instead, the intellect knows the nature of the external thing through the species.

This gives Aquinas an intentional realism: cognition is mediated by formal likeness but directed to things. The mental word, concept, definition, proposition, or judgment is formed by the intellect in act, but the mature cognitive act is ordered toward reality. Words signify what the intellect forms in order to judge external things, not merely the species considered as private mental items. Contemporary scholarship continues to debate how “direct realist” Aquinas is, but his anti-veil commitment is clear at the textual level.

Spanish-language work on Aquinas’s intentionality has emphasized this point. Carlos A. Casanova’s article on intentionality and agent intellect summarizes Aquinas as holding that human beings obtain intelligible species from sensible things through the agent intellect, and that judgment moves from species toward the thing through a spiritual contact that transcends the species. That is a good contemporary way to capture Aquinas’s own refusal to treat species as the final object of knowledge.

7. Concept, judgment, reasoning, truth, and error

Aquinas distinguishes simple apprehension, judgment, and reasoning. In simple apprehension the intellect grasps what something is; in judgment it composes or divides, affirming or denying; in reasoning it moves discursively from known to unknown. Human intellect does not usually grasp all truth at once. It works through partial apprehensions, definitions, propositions, and syllogisms. This discursive structure is a mark of human intellectual limitation, not of intellect as such, since Aquinas holds that divine and angelic intellects do not reason in this way.

Truth and falsity enter most properly at the level of judgment. Aquinas holds that intellect is not false with respect to its proper simple grasp of quiddity, considered as such, but falsity can arise in composition and division, and in reasoning. We can misunderstand what follows from what, misattribute a predicate, overgeneralize from a confused grasp, or judge badly because imagination, memory, or bodily disposition is defective. The intellect can understand better or worse partly because the knower’s bodily and internal sensory powers are better or worse disposed.

This matters for a philosophy of mind because Aquinas does not make cognition purely formal or purely bodily. Error can be logical, conceptual, imaginative, memorial, affective, or bodily in its conditions. Sleep, sensory suspension, and disturbed imagination can hinder judgment; Aquinas uses such cases to show that even acquired intellectual knowledge normally requires the cooperation of sensory powers when actually exercised in embodied life.

8. Universals, singulars, and the return to phantasms

Aquinas says the human intellect directly knows universals, not material singulars as singulars. This does not mean we never know individuals. It means that the intellect’s proper act is universal, while knowledge of this individual here and now requires a return to phantasms and cooperation with sensitive powers. In practical life, intellect and sense operate together: I know “human being” universally through intellect, but I encounter and act toward this person through sense, imagination, cogitative power, and memory.

This avoids two opposite distortions. Aquinas does not reduce thought to sensory particularity, because universals are genuinely intellectual. But he also does not treat human thought as detached from concrete particulars, because the intellect must turn back to phantasms to apply universals. Moral reasoning, teaching, medicine, law, and ordinary prudence all require this descent from universal to particular through the cogitative and memorial powers.

9. Self-knowledge and consciousness

Aquinas’s account of self-knowledge is neither Cartesian transparency nor eliminative externalism. The human mind does not first know itself by direct intuition of its essence. Because the human intellect begins in potency, it becomes actually intelligible to itself through its acts. First it knows an object; then it knows that it understands; then, reflectively, it can know its own power and nature. Habits are likewise known through acts: one knows one has faith by knowing one believes.

This gives Aquinas a layered account of consciousness. There is sensory second-order awareness, as common sense allows one to perceive that one sees. There is intellectual reflexivity, by which intellect knows its own act. There is also knowledge of the will’s acts, because willing is an intelligible inclination following an understood good, and intellect and will are rooted in the same soul.

Portuguese scholarship has noticed the contemporary relevance of this doctrine. A Brazilian article on “mind and self-knowledge in Aquinas” frames Aquinas as offering an early philosophical psychology of mind, while stressing that the mind is not self-knowing by a fundamental intuition but must know what it is by considering its object and acts. This is close to Aquinas’s own claim that the human intellect knows itself through its operation rather than by immediate possession of its essence as object.

10. Knowledge of immaterial things: the upward limit of human cognition

The human intellect’s natural object in this life is the quiddity of material things. Therefore it does not naturally and directly know immaterial substances—God, angels, or even the soul’s own essence—in the way it knows material natures. It can know something about immaterial realities by negation, analogy, causation, and comparison with material things. We can know that God exists and that certain attributes must be said of God, but we do not naturally know God’s essence. God is not the first object naturally known by the human mind.

This is a discipline of intellectual humility. Aquinas’s apophatic and analogical method is not anti-intellectual. It is a theory of proportion: a created embodied intellect can know beyond matter, but its natural route begins in matter. SEP highlights Aquinas’s insistence that we can truly speak of God analogically, while also acknowledging that the divine “higher mode” remains beyond our adequate comprehension.

11. Will: mind includes rational appetite

For Aquinas, mind is not only cognition. The will is an intellectual appetite, and it belongs intrinsically to the higher part of the soul. Appetite is inclination following form; in cognitive beings, appetite follows apprehension. A thing is desired as apprehended under the aspect of good. Sensitive appetite follows sensory apprehension; will follows intellectual apprehension of good.

The will is not simply a desire-module independent of intellect. Intellect presents the good; will tends toward it. Yet will is not a passive slave of cognition. Aquinas famously says intellect moves will by presenting the end or intelligible good, while will moves intellect and the other powers as an agent moves them toward the end. I think because I will to think; I deliberate because I care about an end. Intellect and will mutually include one another’s acts: intellect can understand that will wills, and will can will intellect to understand.

The will necessarily wills happiness or good in general, but it is not necessitated toward every particular good. Finite goods can be considered under different aspects, and reason can deliberate among alternatives. This is the basis of free choice. Aquinas identifies free choice not as a separate faculty but as will in its choosing function, involving counsel and judgment but principally appetitive because choice is acceptance of one option as good.

Aquinas’s freedom is neither randomness nor absolute self-creation. He holds that God is the first cause who moves natural and voluntary causes, but voluntary causes remain voluntary. Temperament, habit, passion, and bodily disposition incline but do not simply necessitate because reason can judge and redirect. SEP notes a continuing scholarly controversy over how to classify Aquinas on determinism, but it also emphasizes his insistence that the will is not necessitated by the intellect’s judgment in ordinary finite choices.

12. Passions, emotion, and the political rule of reason

Aquinas’s theory of mind includes the passions because human agency is embodied. The sensitive appetite is divided into concupiscible and irascible powers. The concupiscible power concerns ordinary sensible goods and evils: attraction, aversion, enjoyment, sorrow. The irascible power concerns arduous goods and evils: danger, difficulty, resistance, hope, fear, daring, anger. Aquinas grounds this division in the fact that animals need not only to seek the suitable and flee the harmful, but also to resist obstacles.

Reason does not dominate the passions despotically, as a master moves an inert tool. Aquinas says reason rules the sensitive appetite politically or royally: the passions have something of their own and can resist. They are moved not only by reason but by imagination and sense. Nevertheless, because the human cogitative power is guided by universal reason, reason can modify or arouse anger, fear, and similar passions by presenting things differently. This is a psychologically realistic account of emotion regulation: affect is rationally educable but not mechanically obedient.

This also explains why virtue matters for mind. A habit is a stable disposition of a power. Intellectual and moral habits shape what one notices, how one judges, what one imagines as salient, how passions arise, and what one chooses. Synderesis is not a power but a natural habit of first practical principles; conscience is not a separate faculty but an act applying knowledge to a case. Aquinas’s philosophy of mind is therefore inseparable from his theory of habit, virtue, and practical reason.

13. The divine mind: not a theological distraction, but the summit of the theory

Aquinas’s treatment of God’s knowledge is not an optional theological appendix. It is the highest case of his general principle that cognition corresponds to immateriality. Cognitive beings can have the forms of others; God, as maximally immaterial and pure act, has maximal knowledge. But one must not imagine God as a very large human mind with faculties, representations, and sequential thoughts. In God, intellect, act of understanding, essence, and existence are identical. God’s knowledge is not a quality or habit added to God; it is God’s own substance.

God first and perfectly knows Himself. Since God’s essence is pure actuality and is fully intelligible to Himself, God comprehends Himself, though no creature can comprehend Him. God does not learn, infer, abstract, or receive species from creatures. His knowledge of creatures is grounded in His knowledge of His own essence as the cause and exemplar of all that can be.

Aquinas’s doctrine of divine ideas is central here. Ideas exist in the divine mind as forms or exemplars according to which things are made and known. But because God is simple, the plurality of ideas does not mean a plurality of parts in God. The divine essence is one, but can be understood as imitable in many ways. Aquinas explicitly distinguishes the idea as what God understands from the divine essence as that by which God understands.

God knows singulars, not merely universals. Human intellect abstracts from individuating material conditions and therefore directly knows universals; God does not abstract from creatures. God’s causal knowledge extends to matter and individuating principles themselves, so divine knowledge includes singular material things. God also knows all actual and possible things, finite and infinite multitudes, not successively but in one eternal act.

God’s knowledge is causative when joined with will. Aquinas compares divine knowledge to the artisan’s knowledge, but the analogy must be elevated: God’s knowledge is not copied from things; things are created according to divine wisdom and will. Yet this does not mean that creaturely causality is unreal. Aquinas’s whole metaphysics of creation allows secondary causes to act according to their own natures. Divine knowledge grounds the order of things without making creatures mere illusions.

The hardest case is future contingents. Aquinas holds that God knows future contingent events because all times are present to divine eternity. But those events are not thereby necessary in their proximate causes. A future free act can be contingently caused in time and eternally known by God. Whether one accepts the theological framework or not, this is a profound theory of how omniscience and contingency might coexist: divine knowledge is not temporal prediction from prior evidence but eternal presence to the whole order of being.

14. The beatific vision and the outer boundary of created mind

Aquinas’s doctrine of the beatific vision also belongs to philosophy of mind because it concerns the maximum possible elevation of created intellect. No created likeness or representation can make God’s essence adequately known. To see God’s essence, the created intellect must be strengthened by the light of glory, and the divine essence itself must be united to the intellect as the object seen. This is not natural human cognition perfected by better abstraction; it is a supernatural elevation of intellect beyond its natural proportion.

Even then, the creature does not comprehend God. Seeing God does not mean knowing all that God knows, nor exhausting the infinite divine essence. Creatures can see the same divine essence more or less perfectly according to the degree of light and capacity given, but no finite intellect becomes infinite.

This is important for Aquinas’s hierarchy of mind. Human natural cognition begins from sense; angelic cognition is naturally intellectual and non-embodied; divine cognition is identical with divine being; beatified cognition is a created participation in the direct vision of God. The system is not flat. It is a graded metaphysics of intellectual life.

15. Angelic minds: finite disembodied intellects

Aquinas’s angelology is philosophically serious because angels are his chief case of finite non-embodied minds. Angels are not divine; they are creatures. Unlike God, an angel’s act of understanding is not identical with its substance. Unlike humans, angels have no naturally united bodies and therefore no senses, imagination, nutrition, or bodily passions. They are called intellects or minds because all their knowledge is intellectual. Their powers are intellect and will.

Because angels do not have bodies, they do not abstract intelligible species from phantasms. Aquinas therefore denies that they have agent and passive intellect in the human sense. Human intellect needs an agent intellect because material phantasms must be made actually intelligible. Angelic intellect receives connatural intelligible species from God along with its intellectual nature.

Angelic knowledge is finite. God knows all things through His one essence; angels need species. Higher angels know through fewer and more universal species; lower angels through more numerous and less universal species. These universal angelic species are not universals abstracted from particulars, but created intelligible forms by which many things can be known properly. This is a key contrast with human cognition: the same word “universal” functions differently in embodied abstraction and in angelic intellectual species.

An angel knows itself through its own essence because it is a subsistent intelligible form actually present to itself. It knows other angels and material things through impressed species. It can know material things intellectually, not sensorially, and can know singulars because its species can represent individuating principles as well as forms. This is not human singular knowledge through phantasms, but an intellectual knowledge of singulars by a higher unified power.

Angelic knowledge has limits. Angels do not naturally know future contingents as future contingents; they know them only insofar as they are contained in causes or revealed. They do not automatically know the secret acts of human will, because intellect and will are not simply readable from bodily or imaginative states. They do not comprehend all mysteries of grace unless revealed, even if blessed angels see God.

Angels do not reason discursively as humans do. They can understand propositions and syllogisms, but they do not move from unknown to known by syllogistic inquiry. Their intellect grasps at once what is virtually contained in what they know. They do not compose and divide in the human way, because they do not need to assemble partial concepts into judgments through a temporal process. Natural angelic knowledge is not false in itself, though Aquinas allows that demons can err regarding supernatural matters because of disordered will and limited revelation.

Aquinas’s distinction between morning and evening knowledge is also philosophically significant. Morning knowledge is knowledge of things in the Word; evening knowledge is knowledge of things in their own created nature. This is not a decorative theological metaphor. It marks two standpoints: knowing creatures in the first creative source, and knowing them in their own finite mode. Aquinas thus treats angelic knowledge as both metaphysically vertical and creaturely finite.

16. The separated human soul: not angel, not complete human person

Aquinas’s account of the separated soul is a boundary case between human and angelic mind. In embodied life, the soul understands through phantasms. After separation from the body, it can understand, but by a different and less natural mode: through species participated from divine light rather than through sensory images. This does not show that the body was unnecessary. Aquinas explicitly says the soul’s union with the body is for the soul’s good, and separation is not natural to it.

The separated soul knows itself through itself, knows other separated souls more directly, and knows angels imperfectly. Its knowledge of natural material things is general and confused compared with embodied human knowledge. This is decisive against the notion that Aquinas secretly thinks the body is a prison. The disembodied human soul survives, but its proper natural mode is not angelic; it is a wounded or incomplete mode awaiting bodily restoration in the theological horizon of resurrection.

17. What Aquinas rejects

Aquinas rejects materialism because intellectual understanding has universal, immaterial content and is not the act of a bodily organ. He rejects Cartesian dualism because the human being is not two complete substances, body and mind, externally related; the soul is the form of the body. He rejects Platonism insofar as it makes the body unnecessary for human intellectual acquisition. He rejects Avicennian or illuminationist accounts when they make separate intelligences the ordinary proximate source of human intelligible species. He rejects Averroist monopsychism because each human being has an individual intellective soul; otherwise Socrates and Plato would not truly be distinct understanders.

He also rejects a veil-of-ideas theory in which we know only our mental representations. The intelligible species is the principle by which we know, not the primary object known. He rejects pure introspectionism because the human mind knows itself through acts. He rejects affective irrationalism because passions can be guided by reason, but he also rejects simplistic rational control because passions obey politically and can resist.

18. The extracted Aquinian philosophy of mind in theses

A comprehensive Aquinian philosophy of mind can be stated in twelve theses.

First, mind belongs within a metaphysics of life and form. Soul is not an occult object added to a body but the actuality by which a living thing is alive. Human mind is rooted in the intellectual soul as substantial form.

Second, human beings are unified substances, not mind-body aggregates. The same form accounts for human bodily life, sensation, and understanding. This is why Aquinas’s position is a hylomorphic unity rather than body-soul juxtaposition.

Third, intellectual operation is immaterial but embodied in its normal human exercise. The intellect is not a bodily organ, yet actual human thinking in this life depends on phantasms and therefore on sensory and brain-involving powers.

Fourth, intentionality is formal possession without material assimilation. Cognition occurs when the knower receives the form of another according to the knower’s mode. This is the metaphysical basis of representation, directness, and object-directedness.

Fifth, human cognition is bottom-up and top-down. It begins in sense, but agent intellect actively abstracts intelligible species; universal reason then guides the cogitative power and sensitive appetite in practical life.

Sixth, species are cognitive media, not private objects. The mind does not primarily know its own internal likenesses; it knows things through likenesses. Aquinas thus has a representational vocabulary without a representationalist prison.

Seventh, self-knowledge is reflexive and act-based. The human mind does not begin with transparent possession of itself; it knows itself through knowing its acts, habits, and objects.

Eighth, will is part of mind. The rational appetite is not reducible to cognition but is inseparable from it. Intellect presents the good; will moves powers toward the good; free choice emerges from this interplay.

Ninth, emotion is intelligent but not intellectual. Passions belong to sensitive appetite and can be guided by reason through imagination and cogitative appraisal, but they retain their own dynamism.

Tenth, mind is hierarchical by degree of immateriality. Human mind is embodied intellectuality; angelic mind is finite disembodied intellectuality; divine mind is pure self-identical understanding. This hierarchy is not ornamental but explanatory.

Eleventh, created intellect is finite even at its highest. Angels, separated souls, and beatified humans are not God. They can be elevated, but no created intellect comprehends the infinite divine essence.

Twelfth, the philosophy of mind is inseparable from metaphysics, epistemology, and ethics. For Aquinas, to understand mind is to understand being, truth, good, action, habit, and finality. Mind is ordered not merely to representation but to truth and good.

19. Multilingual reception: what non-English discussions highlight

A scan of French, Italian, German, Spanish, and Portuguese sources shows that non-English reception often emphasizes dimensions sometimes flattened in Anglophone “mind-body problem” framing. French discussions, for example, strongly stress Aquinas’s anti-Platonic unity of the human being. Joseph Moreau’s French article frames Aquinas as distinguishing intellectual from sensory knowledge while refusing to make sensation a purely soul-act detached from bodily organ modification. Emmanuel Housset’s French work emphasizes that starting from isolated “body,” “soul,” or “spirit” obscures the original unity of the human person, and cites Aquinas’s description of the intellectual soul as a horizon between corporeal and incorporeal realities.

Italian reference sources put strong weight on Aquinas’s anti-Averroism and the individuality of understanding. Treccani describes his psychology as a passage from potency to act: external sensation, internal sense, fantasy, abstraction by agent intellect, and actualization of possible intellect. It also stresses that Aquinas’s polemic against Averroism is meant to preserve the fact that “this human being” understands, not a single separated intellect for the whole species.

Italian theological-philosophical summaries also highlight the integrity of the human being. One Italian account of the soul as form of the body stresses that the principle making the human being think is the same principle making the human being and the human body exist, and that the human soul is the single form responsible for vegetative, sensitive, and rational functions. The DISF Italian entry similarly describes Aquinas as having an integral vision of the human being: soul and body, intellect and will.

German reception, represented in the Metzler/Spektrum entry, emphasizes openness to being and embodied sense-experience together. It presents Aquinas’s human person as the unity of spiritual soul and matter, a “spirit-ensouled body,” not a collapse into two substances. It also notes that the human being is open to all beings and regions of being, while this openness begins in the sensibly given object.

Spanish and Latin American discussions often focus on intentionality and the agent intellect. Casanova’s Spanish article treats Aquinas’s agent intellect as explaining how intelligible species are obtained from sensible things, while judgment reaches through species to the thing itself. Dialnet-indexed Spanish scholarship by Juan Fernando Sellés frames the agent intellect as a central issue in Aquinas’s theory of human knowledge and argues for its personal character in each human being, though that interpretive emphasis is not universally shared.

Portuguese-language work likewise stresses the intellectual soul as form of the body and the plurality of faculties. Nadir Antonio Pichler’s article states that its purpose is to characterize the human soul as an intellectual power united to the body as form, and it explicitly lists passive intellect, agent intellect, memory, intelligence, speculative and practical intellect, and conscience within Aquinas’s psychology and epistemology. This maps well onto an extraction of Aquinas as a full philosophy of mind rather than a single doctrine of “immortality.”

20. Contemporary philosophical significance

Aquinas’s account is unusually resistant to modern binaries. Against reductive physicalism, he argues that universal intellectual content cannot be the act of a bodily organ. Against disembodied rationalism, he argues that human thought in this life requires phantasms and is impaired when sensory and imaginative powers are impaired. Against representationalism, he makes species the means rather than the object of thought. Against simple direct realism, he gives an elaborate account of formal cognitive mediation. Against voluntarism, he gives intellect priority in presenting the good; against intellectualism without agency, he gives will a real power to move intellect and other powers.

In modern terms, Aquinas can be read as offering an embodied but non-reductive philosophy of mind; a realist theory of intentionality; a layered faculty psychology; a theory of affective-cognitive integration; and an account of self-knowledge that avoids both Cartesian immediacy and third-person reduction. His angelology supplies a model of finite non-embodied cognition; his theology of divine knowledge supplies the limiting case of pure, non-receptive, non-discursive knowledge; his doctrine of the beatific vision supplies the possibility of created intellectual elevation beyond natural proportion. These are not detachable curiosities. They are part of the architecture that makes the human case intelligible by contrast.

The most compressed formula for Aquinas’s philosophy of mind is this: human mind is the intellectual life of an embodied form, naturally knowing material being through sense-dependent abstraction, reflexively knowing itself through its acts, freely tending toward the good through will, and standing within a hierarchy of intellectual life whose summit is God’s simple self-knowledge and whose finite disembodied case is the angelic intellect.

Primary texts most central for further study are Summa theologiae I, qq. 75–89 on the human soul and cognition; I, qq. 14–15 on divine knowledge and ideas; I, qq. 54–59 on angelic intellect and will; I, qq. 80–83 on appetite, will, and free choice; I-II, qq. 22–48 on passions; De veritate qq. 8–10 on angelic knowledge, communication, and mind; Quaestiones disputatae de anima; De unitate intellectus contra Averroistas; and Aquinas’s commentary on Aristotle’s De anima. The Corpus Thomisticum provides the Latin Leonine-based text, while New Advent provides the older English Dominican translation used in many of the citations above.

PreviousBonaventure NextHenry of Ghent

Digital Minds · A history of thinking about the mind, from antiquity to the present day.
A personal research project by Fabián. · About · Glossary
